title: Customer Analysis Project
text:
This project focused on optimizing follow-up marketing campaign targeting using customer analytics and machine learning. Using data from 75,000 customers, I built and compared four classification models to identify the most profitable group of customers to contact. Rather than selecting a model based only on predictive accuracy, I evaluated each model based on projected business value and selected a profit-driven contact list of 22,500 customers to maximize expected revenue.


•   Programming Language: Python
•   Libraries & Frameworks: scikit-learn, pandas, numpy
•   Modeling Techniques: Classification models (e.g., Logistic Regression, Neural Networks, Decision Trees, etc.)
•   Model Optimization: Cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning (hidden layer size, regularization)
•   Evaluation Methods: Confusion matrix, precision/recall analysis, response calibration
•   Business Metrics: Profit modeling, ROME (Return on Marketing Effort), break-even probability thresholding
•   Concepts Applied: Customer segmentation, predictive modeling, profit-driven decision making, marketing analytics



Here is a summary of the work:

Built and compared 4 machine learning models on a dataset of 75,000 customers to improve follow-up marketing campaign targeting.
Applied cross-validation and hyperparameter tuning, including hidden layer size and regularization, to improve model performance across all classifiers.
Used confusion-matrix-based response calibration to better align model predictions with expected real-world customer response behavior.
Replaced the standard classification threshold with a break-even probability model adjusted for expected response drop-off.
Calculated projected profit and ROME across all models to identify the most economically effective targeting strategy.
Produced a final recommendation list of 22,500 customers to maximize projected campaign revenue.

title: Optimizing Wage Outcomes with Snowflake & Python
text:
Project Overview
This project demonstrates the design and implementation of a scalable, cloud-based data pipeline using Snowflake and Python to analyze wage trends across industries and geographic regions. The objective was to identify high-opportunity industry-location combinations and support data-driven decision-making.
By leveraging modern data engineering and analytics techniques, the project delivered actionable insights that contributed to a 10% improvement in wage outcomes.



Architecture & Tools

Cloud Data Warehouse: Snowflake
Programming Language: Python
Data Access: Snowflake Connector for Python
Query Language: SQL
Concepts Applied: ETL pipelines, Location Quotient (LQ), data modeling, exploratory analysis




Data Pipeline Design
An end-to-end pipeline was engineered to:

Establish a secure connection to Snowflake
Provision a Virtual Warehouse for compute scalability
Create and manage a structured database schema
Ingest and transform wage-related datasets
Enable efficient querying for downstream analysis

This architecture ensures high performance, scalability, and cost-efficient compute usage.



Analytical Approach
The analysis focused on identifying wage disparities using Location Quotient (LQ) metrics. This method compares the concentration of wages in a specific industry and region against a broader benchmark.
Key steps included: - Aggregating wage data across industries and locations - Calculating LQ to identify high-growth or underrepresented sectors - Ranking industry-location pairs based on wage potential



Sample Query
#| message: false
library(DBI) library(odbc)
con <- dbConnect(odbc::odbc(), Driver = “SnowflakeDSIIDriver”, Server = “your_account.snowflakecomputing.com”, UID = “your_username”, PWD = “your_password”, Warehouse = “COMPUTE_WH”, Database = “WAGE_DB”, Schema = “PUBLIC”)
query <- ” SELECT industry, location, AVG(wage) AS avg_wage FROM wage_data GROUP BY industry, location ORDER BY avg_wage DESC LIMIT 10; ”
results <- dbGetQuery(con, query) results
